unitTitle: KS1 Y2 Digital photography (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Taking photographs
lessons.objectives:
I can name devices that can take photographs
I can hold a device to take a photograph
I can take a photograph with a digital device
I can say what I did to take my photograph
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on the board: device · camera · photograph · capture · image · digital. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; devices ready (tablet / camera if available). Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — can these take photos? On the starter worksheet pupils SORT devices into "can take photos / cannot take photos". A device needs a camera to take a photo.
   • Support: choose the one that can take a photo.  • Core: a device that takes photos must have a camera.  • Challenge: name a device in the room that can take a photo.
2. (8–18) I-do — photograph vs drawing; a photograph is captured with a camera. Then the three steps: Hold · Look · Press. Likely error: not looking at the screen in the excitement → fix-words "look first, then press."
3. (18–35) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the three steps, then do the photo challenge (a building, a close-up, something far away, a selfie). Always ask before photographing a person; check the background.
4. (35–50) We-do — pupils choose their best photo and photograph/screenshot it for show-your-work.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil says one thing they did to take their photo.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Landscape or portrait?
lessons.objectives:
I can say what portrait and landscape mean
I can take a photo in portrait format
I can take a photo in landscape format
I can say why a photo looks better one way
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: landscape · portrait. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; devices ready. Recap: hold · look · press. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — two ways to hold the device. On the starter worksheet pupils learn portrait (upright, taller than wide) and landscape (sideways, wider than tall) and choose which is which.
   • Support: choose which is held upright.  • Core: a landscape photo is wider than tall.  • Challenge: when would you hold it sideways?
2. (8–25) I-do → we-do — portrait suits tall things (a person, a tower); landscape suits wide things (a beach, mountains, a group). On the activity worksheet pupils SORT subjects into portrait / landscape. Likely error: mixing the words → fix-words "portrait = a person standing up, tall."
3. (25–45) You-do — choose a subject, PREDICT the best format, take it both ways, decide which is best (delete the weaker one). Ask before photographing a person.
4. (45–50) We-do — pupils photograph/screenshot their best photo for show-your-work and say which format won.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil says why their photo is better that way.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What makes a good photograph?
lessons.objectives:
I can spot what is wrong with a photo
I can say how to take a good photo
I can take a well-composed photo
I can improve a photo by taking it again
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: framing · subject · compose. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; devices and the Paper frame ready. Recap: portrait and landscape. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — which photos need improving? On the starter worksheet pupils SORT photos into good / needs improving (too close, too far, blurry).
   • Support: choose the good photo.  • Core: say why the goat photo is not good.  • Challenge: name one thing that makes a photo hard to see.
2. (8–20) I-do — three things to check: positioning, framing, detail. Likely error: cutting off part of the subject → fix-words "fit the whole thing in the frame."
3. (20–40) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to compose a photo, then take a display, a window view and some pencils, and rate each one.
4. (40–50) We-do — pupils choose their lowest-rated photo, take it again better, and photograph/screenshot the improved one.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; pairs share a before/after and say what changed.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Lighting
lessons.objectives:
I can see how light changes a photo
I can find a place with good light
I can use a different light source
I can say why a photo is unclear
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: light source · flash · focus · background. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; devices and a torch/lamp ready, auto-flash off. Recap: positioning, framing, detail. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — keep or delete? On the starter worksheet pupils SORT photos into keep (good light) / delete (too dark).
   • Support: choose the photo to keep.  • Core: a photo is hard to see when there is not enough light.  • Challenge: why might a photo turn out too dark?
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — cameras need enough light; check the light before you press. Pupils photograph the SAME object in different places and rate how clear each is (windowsill, under a table, the hall). Likely error: changing the object too → fix-words "same object, only the light changes."
3. (22–42) You-do — add a light source. On the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to fix a dark photo (torch or flash), then try it. Likely error: covering the flash with a finger → fix-words "keep fingers away from the flash."
4. (42–50) We-do — pupils photograph/screenshot their clearest photo and say which place was best.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one way to fix a dark photo.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Effects
lessons.objectives:
I can see that a photo can be changed
I can use a tool to change the colour
I can save my edited photo with a clear name
I can explain my choice
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: editing · filter. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the photo editor open with a photo to edit. Recap: lighting and clear photos. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — the same photo, changed. On the starter worksheet pupils MATCH a colour effect to how it feels (blue tint = cold, red tint = warm). Editing = changing a photo on a computer.
   • Support: can a photo be changed? yes/no.  • Core: changing a photo on a computer is called editing.  • Challenge: what colour effect makes a photo feel warm?
2. (8–18) I-do — open the photo, find the Adjust tools, move a colour slider. Likely error: dragging every slider at once → fix-words "one slider, then look."
3. (18–38) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to edit and save, then change the colour of their own photo.
4. (38–50) We-do — Save as a copy with a clear name; pupils photograph/screenshot their edited photo. Likely error: overwriting the original → fix-words "Save as a copy, not Save."
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil says what they changed and why.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Is it real?
lessons.objectives:
I can use my photo skills to take a good photo
I can see that photos can be changed
I can spot clues that a photo has been changed
I can decide if a photo is real or changed
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: format · framing · lighting · focus · filter. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; devices and the Paper frame ready. This is the last lesson — recap the whole unit. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — remember your photo skills. On the starter worksheet pupils TICK everything to think about when taking a photo (format, framing, lighting — not the colour of your shoes).
   • Support: should you check the lighting? yes/no.  • Core: what does framing mean?  • Challenge: write two things you check.
2. (8–23) You-do (Activity 1) — pupils take their best photo of the classroom using every skill from the unit. Ask before photographing a person; check the background.
3. (23–40) We-do — real or changed? On the activity worksheet pupils SORT photos into real / changed and spot a clue (snow in one part but not another; something too big or in the wrong place). Likely error: thinking a changed photo is real → fix-words "look for the clue that does not fit."
4. (40–50) Plenary — pupils decide real/changed on a few examples and photograph/screenshot their best photo for show-your-work.
5. (50–55) Self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names one clue that a photo has been changed. End by celebrating the best photos.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
